title: EU Incorporated: The way to start-up European competitiveness – European Council on Foreign Relations
gist: Europe matches the US and China in startup creation but loses at the scaling stage due to 27 fragmented national markets; the EU's proposed 'EU Inc.' 28th-regime corporate form aims to fix this, and the author argues it must pass as a binding regulation, not a diluted directive, to avoid a first-order macroeconomic failure.

themes.theme: The US-Europe scaling gap in hard numbers

insights.claim: In 2024, venture investment rounds above $100m totalled $120.8bn in the US versus just $18.1bn in Europe, exposing a ~7x gap concentrated at the growth/scaling stage.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: Quantifies where European startups die (scale-up funding), the exact chokepoint any EU competitiveness policy or late-stage fund must target.
insights.quote: In 2024, investment rounds above $100m totalled $120.8bn in the US compared to just $18.1bn in Europe.
insights.locator: The problem arises at the scaling stage

insights.claim: In 2025 deep tech investment reached $130bn in the US versus just $16bn in Europe, with the gap widening further in AI and other breakthrough technologies.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: The ~8x deep tech gap signals Europe is being structurally locked out of the highest-value frontier sectors, a long-run drag on European tech equities.
insights.locator: The problem arises at the scaling stage

insights.claim: Europe's share of global tech market value has collapsed from 30% in 2000 to just 7% today, even as tech now accounts for 15% of European GDP versus 4% in 2015.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: A sector rising to 15% of GDP while losing global value share means Europe captures the demand but not the equity upside — a structural underweight case.
insights.quote: Europe’s share of global tech market value has plummeted from 30% in 2000 to just 7% today
insights.locator: Over time, these dynamics have led to a structural decline

insights.claim: Since 2015 Europe has produced more start-ups than America and matches Asia's share of new founders, with talent costs below the Bay Area or New York and advanced positions in quantum, climate tech and biotech.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: Europe's problem is not idea or founder supply but conversion; cheaper talent and deep-tech strength are the underpriced asset if scaling frictions ease.
insights.locator: In some ways, Europe is not too far behind

themes.theme: Why capital and companies leave Europe

insights.claim: Nearly half of Europe's growth-stage funding last year came from abroad, predominantly America, and those investors often push founders to relocate their companies once they reach scale.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: Foreign-dominated late-stage capital creates a built-in redomiciliation pipeline; European IPO supply leaks to US exchanges structurally.
insights.locator: A lack of European money means that much capital comes from overseas

insights.claim: European institutional capital such as pension funds chronically underinvests in venture capital, and the institutional culture prices risk more conservatively than America's, starving late-stage rounds.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: Pension-fund VC allocation is the policy lever to watch; any mandate shift would be a large, sticky new capital source for European scale-ups.
insights.locator: It would be misleading to only blame regulatory fragmentation

insights.claim: The fragmentation of 27 member-state markets and its regulatory complexity acts as an invisible ceiling that manifests specifically at the scaling stage, driving 'high-growth forum shopping' that drains talent, jobs and prior public investment abroad.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: Frames fragmentation as a scale-stage tax, meaning fixes must target cross-border operation, not early-stage subsidies.
insights.locator: The problem arises at the scaling stage

insights.claim: The European Commission has moved to approve 'EU Inc.' (the 28th regime), a harmonised, optional, automatically recognised and fully digital corporate entity covering commercial, tax, insolvency and labour law so startups operate under one set of rules instead of 27.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: A single EU corporate form is the concrete regulatory catalyst; its passage terms determine whether European scale-ups stay domiciled in the bloc.

insights.claim: In June 2026 the Council's own legal service advised national delegations that the EU Inc. proposal may need to be downgraded from a regulation to a directive, which the author warns would undermine its harmonising ambition from the outset.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: The regulation-vs-directive fight is the single binary that decides if EU Inc. is real harmonisation or a hollow standard — the key event to watch to 2027.
insights.locator: Second, in the complex institutional negotiations

insights.claim: The author argues that if the 28th regime derails under pressure from national interests it would be a macroeconomic failure of the first order.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: Elevates a corporate-law file to macro-critical status; investors in European tech should treat its outcome as a structural, not niche, signal.
insights.quote: Should the 28th regime derail under pressure from national interests, it would be a macroeconomic failure of the first order

insights.claim: EU Inc.'s success depends on parallel progress across other files: the startup and scaleup strategy, a European Innovation Act, the capital markets union, and reform of merger guidelines.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: The corporate form alone is insufficient; capital markets union and merger-rule reform are the complementary catalysts to monitor.
insights.locator: First, the regime can only reach its full potential

themes.theme: National vetoes and the dilution risk

insights.claim: Germany is expected to scrutinise any framework that bypasses its co-determination model (Mitbestimmung), France to defend its national-champions agenda, and the Nordics to protect favourable tax treatment of stock options and employee share schemes.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: Names the exact national veto points that could dilute EU Inc.; each is a concrete negotiation flashpoint that could stall the file.
insights.locator: Second, in the complex institutional negotiations

insights.claim: New EU Inc. legislation must include tax measures to prevent imbalances favouring member states with more attractive tax laws such as Ireland, Luxembourg or the Netherlands.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: Without tax harmonisation, EU Inc. could accelerate intra-EU domicile arbitrage toward low-tax hubs rather than curb offshoring.
insights.locator: Within this, the new legislation must include tax measures

insights.claim: If negotiations are blocked, the author urges parallel levers: von der Leyen's April 2026 proposed move away from unanimity voting plus fast-track mutual recognition mechanisms between member states, modelled on the European Competitiveness Lab.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: A shift away from unanimity voting is the escape hatch; its emergence would signal Brussels is serious about forcing competitiveness reform through.
insights.locator: Moreover, in case of an effective blockage

insights.claim: The author models EU Inc. on Airbus and Galileo, both of which combined a single legal framework, pooled public investment and continental scale to overcome national fragmentation and become global-scale champions.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: Provides the template investors can use to judge whether EU Inc. has the ingredients (common law + pooled capital + scale) to succeed.
insights.locator: The EU can do it again

insights.claim: Galileo required more than €10bn of joint EU-budget funding over two decades and is widely credited with pushing Washington to end deliberate GPS civilian-use restrictions.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: Historical proof that pooled EU infrastructure spend can force US strategic concessions — the geopolitical payoff case for sovereignty investment.
insights.locator: And in the 2000s, Galileo gave Europe

insights.claim: The author proposes a research-to-market chain where AI factories and the EuroHPC joint undertaking supply scientific infrastructure, EU Inc. is the legal vehicle, and the European Innovation Council and investment bank act as the financing bridge from lab to market.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: Maps the concrete pipeline for turning EU research into companies; spin-offs born as EU Incs are the assets to watch if the stack aligns.
insights.locator: Europe has networks of academic research centres

themes.theme: US strategic self-harm as Europe's opening

insights.claim: Trump-era tariff volatility, political pressure on independent agencies, restrictions on foreign researchers and politicised investment decisions are eroding America's core competitive assets of predictability and legal certainty.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: Higher US business risk is the exogenous shock that hands Europe a first-in-decades window to attract emerging tech companies on rule-of-law grounds.
insights.quote: President Donald Trump’s rule is now eroding some of America’s most competitive assets: predictability and legal certainty.
insights.locator: America has long been the go-to home

insights.claim: For the first time in decades Europe can compete for emerging companies by offering stable institutions, clear rules, independent regulators and a rule-of-law guarantee as an alternative to a riskier America.
insights.speaker: Miguel Ferrer
insights.so_what: Positions rule-of-law stability as Europe's differentiated pitch; the thesis only pays off if EU Inc. and capital reforms actually land.
insights.locator: For the first time in decades
